main_ingredients: food acids (e.g. phosphoric acid or citric acid)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-serve can of soda is approximately 330 ml (roughly 330 g), which is the most common retail serving size consumed in Singapore and globally.

nutrient_source.title: Carbonated beverage, cola — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174851); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Food Composition Database (Soda entry, cr_id G00010987)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: The HPB source records energy_kcal as 59 kcal/100 g with all macros at 0.0 g, which is nutritionally inconsistent (59 kcal cannot arise from zero protein, fat, and carbohydrate). This is a known data-entry artefact in the HPB record where sugar content was not separately populated. USDA SR Legacy carbonated cola (FDC 174851) reports approximately 10.6 g sugar/100 g and 41 kcal/100 g for a standard cola; however, the HPB energy figure of 59 kcal/100 g is retained as the primary energy anchor per source-priority rules, and sugar_g is estimated at 14.75 g/100 g (59 kcal divided by 4 kcal/g) to resolve the macro-energy inconsistency. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are confirmed zero by product formulation. Micronutrient values for potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, and magnesium_mg are taken directly from the HPB record. Vitamin and trace mineral fields not reported in the HPB record were cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy FDC 174851; those fields also not individually reported in USDA SR Legacy for this generic category are set to -1 as documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Soft drink, Coca-Cola — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283; University of Sydney GI Database entry for cola-type carbonated soft drink.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 63 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for a standard cola-type carbonated soft drink (Coca-Cola reference food). This is used as the closest analogous approved-source entry for generic soda. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (63 x 14.75 g/100g x 330 g serving / 100) = (63 x 48.675) / 100 = approximately 30.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage; estimated 14.75 g sugar per 100 g drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and raises blood pressure independently of dietary sodium intake
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid regular soda entirely, as habitual sugar-sweetened beverage consumption independently elevates blood pressure through insulin-driven renal sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation; replace with plain water, unsweetened sparkling water, or unsweetened herbal teas.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 63) and high glycemic load per serving (~30.7 GL); liquid sugar from carbonated soft drinks is absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes must avoid regular soda, as its high GI (63) and high per-serving glycemic load (~30.7) cause rapid postprandial glucose excursions; liquid sugar from carbonated beverages is absorbed faster than equivalent solid carbohydrates and provides no satiety benefit.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular sugar-sweetened beverage consumption is independently associated with increased risk of cardiovascular events, dyslipidaemia, and visceral adiposity
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should eliminate regular soda from their diet, as habitual sugar-sweetened beverage intake is associated with elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, increased visceral fat, and a higher incidence of major adverse cardiovascular events in prospective cohort studies.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory dietary pattern; no anti-inflammatory nutrients present; phosphoric acid may impair calcium absorption in patients on corticosteroids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid soda as it provides no anti-inflammatory benefit (no omega-3, polyphenols, antioxidants, or fibre), and phosphoric acid in cola-type sodas may reduce calcium absorption, worsening corticosteroid-related bone loss.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soda, also known as a carbonated soft drink or fizzy drink, is a commercially manufactured ultra-processed beverage consisting primarily of carbonated water, sugar or high-fructose corn syrup, food-grade acids (such as phosphoric acid or citric acid), natural or artificial flavourings, and preservatives. It is consumed globally and is widely available in Singapore in standard 330 ml cans and 600 ml bottles. The beverage is characteristically sweet, effervescent, and refreshing in taste, with no significant nutritional value — it provides calories almost exclusively from added sugars and contains negligible protein, fat, dietary fibre, vitamins, or minerals. The Singapore HPB food composition record (cr_id G00010987) reports 59 kcal per 100 g with all macronutrients recorded as zero, which is a known data-entry artefact; the caloric content is consistent with approximately 14.75 g of sugar per 100 g (59 kcal divided by 4 kcal/g). Clinically, soda is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is contraindicated for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes (high GI of 63, high glycemic load per serving of approximately 30.7 for a 330 ml can), hypertension (insulin-mediated sodium retention), cardiovascular disease (associated with dyslipidaemia and visceral adiposity), and rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory, no beneficial nutrients). Soda has no role in a therapeutic diet and should be replaced with plain water, unsweetened sparkling water, or unsweetened herbal teas.
common_user_queries: Can I drink soft drinks if I have high blood pressure?
How much sugar is in soda?
Is carbonated soft drink suitable for heart disease?
What is the glycemic index of soda?
Is fizzy drink okay for rheumatoid arthritis?
How many calories in a can of soda?
What can I drink instead of soda?
Is diet soda better than regular soda?

confidence_reason: The HPB source (cr_id G00010987) is an approved primary source for Singapore food records, but the per_100g macro data contains a known inconsistency (59 kcal with all macros at 0.0 g). Carbohydrate and sugar values have been estimated by back-calculation from energy (59 kcal divided by 4 kcal/g = 14.75 g/100 g) and cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy carbonated cola data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high due to the macro estimation step.
notes: ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): The original HPB record reports protein_g = 0.0, fat_g = 0.0, and carbohydrate_g = 0.0 alongside energy_kcal = 59.0, which is nutritionally inconsistent. Protein_g is confirmed zero — soda contains no protein by formulation. Fat_g is confirmed zero — soda contains no fat by formulation. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all confirmed zero by product formulation. Carbohydrate_g has been corrected from 0.0 to 14.75 g/100 g by back-calculation from energy (59 kcal divided by 4 kcal/g), as the zero value is a data-entry artefact in the HPB record. Sugar_g is set equal to carbohydrate_g (14.75 g/100 g) as all carbohydrate in soda is from added sugars. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero — soda contains no dietary fibre. Cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — soda contains no cholesterol by formulation. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): The following fields are set to -1 because the data is not available in either the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id G00010987) or the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy carbonated cola entry (FDC ID 174851) for this generic soda category: vitamin_c_mg — not reported in HPB record or USDA SR Legacy FDC 174851 for generic carbonated soft drink; vitamin_a_mcg_rae — not reported in HPB record or USDA SR Legacy FDC 174851 for generic carbonated soft drink; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg — not reported in HPB record or USDA SR Legacy FDC 174851 for generic carbonated soft drink; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg — not reported in HPB record or USDA SR Legacy FDC 174851 for generic carbonated soft drink; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg — not reported in HPB record or USDA SR Legacy FDC 174851 for generic carbonated soft drink; vitamin_b6_mg — not reported in HPB record or USDA SR Legacy FDC 174851 for generic carbonated soft drink; vitamin_b12_mcg — not reported in HPB record or USDA SR Legacy FDC 174851 for generic carbonated soft drink; folate_mcg_dfe — not reported in HPB record or USDA SR Legacy FDC 174851 for generic carbonated soft drink; vitamin_d_mcg — not reported in HPB record or USDA SR Legacy FDC 174851 for generic carbonated soft drink; vitamin_e_mg — not reported in HPB record or USDA SR Legacy FDC 174851 for generic carbonated soft drink; phosphorus_mg — not reported in HPB record or USDA SR Legacy FDC 174851 for generic carbonated soft drink; zinc_mg — not reported in HPB record or USDA SR Legacy FDC 174851 for generic carbonated soft drink; selenium_mcg — not reported in HPB record or USDA SR Legacy FDC 174851 for generic carbonated soft drink; water_g — not reported in HPB record or USDA SR Legacy FDC 174851 for generic carbonated soft drink. These fields are expected to be negligible or near-zero for a carbonated sugar-water beverage, but cannot be confirmed zero without explicit source reporting. ONTOLOGY NOTE: FoodOn URI, FoodEx2 code, and SNOMED CT code are cited from published ontology references; reviewers should verify current code validity against live ontology repositories. HPB record cr_id G00010987 classified as record_type dish with food_group Beverages and food_subgroup Soft drinks, preserved in output.
